front: Work: in this play, Prince Segismundo is imprisoned in a tower by his father Basilio, King of Poland, due to a prophecy about his violent nature
back: Life is a Dream (Pedro Calderón de la Barca)

front: Work: in this play, Rosaura arrives and finds a chained prince; Clotaldo recognizes a sword he gave to Rosaura's mother
back: Life is a Dream (Pedro Calderón de la Barca)

front: Work: in this play, Astolfo, who has a claim to the Polish throne, is impelled to marry Rosaura at the end
back: Life is a Dream (Pedro Calderón de la Barca)

front: Work: at the end of this play, Basilio abdicates in favor of Segismundo, who has spared his father's life
back: Life is a Dream (Pedro Calderón de la Barca)

front: Work: in this play, the jester Clarin (Clarion) is shot while hiding behind a rock, revealing that fate cannot be changed
back: Life is a Dream (Pedro Calderón de la Barca)

front: Work: this play opens with a woman arriving near a mountain on a 'wild hippogriff'
back: Life is a Dream (Pedro Calderón de la Barca)

front: Work: while chained and wearing animal skins, a character in this play delivers a soliloquy lamenting that the greatest sin is 'having been born'
back: Life is a Dream (Pedro Calderón de la Barca)

front: Work: Giannina Braschi references this play in United States of Banana, where Segismundo is trapped under the Statue of Liberty
back: Life is a Dream (Pedro Calderón de la Barca)

front: Work: the themes of this play are often compared to Cartesian skepticism, as its protagonist is unsure whether he is awake
back: Life is a Dream (Pedro Calderón de la Barca)

front: Work: the title of this play comes from a conversation between Clotaldo and Segismundo about the nature of reality
back: Life is a Dream (Pedro Calderón de la Barca)

front: Work: Rosaura disguises herself as a man while traveling with Clarin; Astolfo carries a locket with her portrait
back: Life is a Dream (Pedro Calderón de la Barca)

front: Work: Pedro Crespo hangs Don Alvaro for raping his daughter Isabel and is appointed mayor by Philip II
back: The Mayor of Zalamea (Pedro Calderón de la Barca)

front: Work: in this play, Isabel is raped by the soldier Don Alvaro, who is billeted in her father's house
back: The Mayor of Zalamea (Pedro Calderón de la Barca)

front: Work: in this play, Rebolledo stages a mock fight so Don Alvaro can meet Isabel
back: The Mayor of Zalamea (Pedro Calderón de la Barca)

front: Work: in the first act of this play, a soldier's mistress 'La Chispa' sings a marching song beginning 'Titiri tiri'
back: The Mayor of Zalamea (Pedro Calderón de la Barca)

front: Work: this play ends less violently than Calderón's honor plays, as the dishonored Isabella is allowed to become a nun
back: The Mayor of Zalamea (Pedro Calderón de la Barca)

front: Work: Don Gutierre suspects his wife Mencia of infidelity and has a bloodletter bleed her to death
back: The Surgeon of His Honor (Pedro Calderón de la Barca)

front: Work: in this play, Coquin bets King Pedro he can make the king laugh or he'll have his teeth pulled
back: The Surgeon of His Honor (Pedro Calderón de la Barca)

front: Work: in this play, Mencia is suspected of having an affair with Prince Enrique after Gutierre finds a prince's dagger in her bedroom
back: The Surgeon of His Honor (Pedro Calderón de la Barca)

front: Work: in this play, Lisardo is killed in a duel by Eusebio, who is the twin brother of Lisardo's sister Julia
back: Devotion to the Cross (Pedro Calderón de la Barca)

front: Work: in this play, Julia has a scar on her chest that matches Eusebio's
back: Devotion to the Cross (Pedro Calderón de la Barca)

front: Work: in this allegorical auto sacramental, God is represented as the 'Author'
back: The Great Theater of the World (Pedro Calderón de la Barca)

front: Author: this playwright wrote approximately seventy autos sacramentales (one-act allegorical plays)
back: Pedro Calderón de la Barca

front: Author: this Spanish Golden Age playwright's rediscovery began with August Schlegel's translation of five of his plays
back: Pedro Calderón de la Barca

front: Author: the Irish poet Denis Florence MacCarthy was one of the most prominent translators of this playwright
back: Pedro Calderón de la Barca

front: Author: this playwright succeeded Lope de Vega as the leading Spanish Golden Age dramatist
back: Pedro Calderón de la Barca

front: Author: this playwright is known for his 'honor plays' or wife-murder tragedies, including Secret Vengeance for Secret Insult
back: Pedro Calderón de la Barca

front: Author: this playwright's The Hair of Absalom, about Amnon's rape of Tamar, heavily plagiarizes and improves a work by Tirso de Molina
back: Pedro Calderón de la Barca

front: Author: this playwright wrote The Phantom Lady, in which Doña Angela attempts to woo Don Manuel
back: Pedro Calderón de la Barca

front: Author: this playwright retold the Faustian story of Saints Cyprian and Justina
back: Pedro Calderón de la Barca

front: Author: this playwright moderated the excesses of culteranismo in his comedias
back: Pedro Calderón de la Barca
